sections.label: Section 1
sections.original: Οὐκ ἔστιν οὐδὲν δεινὸν ὧδʼ εἰπεῖν ἔπος
sections.translation: There is nothing so terrible to describe , or suffering , or heaven-sent affliction , that human nature may not have to bear the burden of it . The blessed Tantalus — and I am not now taunting him with his misfortunes —

sections.label: Section 5
sections.original: Διὸς πεφυκώς , ὡς λέγουσι , Τάνταλος
sections.translation: Tantalus , the reputed son of Zeus , flies in the air , quailing at the rock which looms above his head ; paying this penalty , they say , for the shameful weakness he displayed in failing to keep a bridle on his lips , when admitted by gods , though he was a man ,

sections.label: Section 10
sections.original: ἀκόλαστον ἔσχε γλῶσσαν , αἰσχίστην νόσον .
sections.translation: to share the honors of their feasts like one of them .

sections.label: Section 11
sections.original: οὗτος φυτεύει Πέλοπα , τοῦ δʼ Ἀτρεὺς ἔφυ ,
sections.translation: He begot Pelops , the father of Atreus , for whom the goddess , when she had carded her wool , spun a web of strife — to make war with his own brother Thyestes . But why need I retrace that hideous tale ?

sections.label: Section 15
sections.original: ἔδαισε δʼ οὖν νιν τέκνʼ ἀποκτείνας Ἀτρεύς .
sections.translation: Well , Atreus slew Thyestes ’ children and feasted him on them . Atreus , now ; I pass over intermediate events ; from Atreus and Aerope of Crete were born the famous Agamemnon , if he really was famous , and Menelaus . Now Menelaus married Helen ,

sections.label: Section 20
sections.original: Μενέλαος Ἑλένην , ὃ δὲ Κλυταιμήστρας λέχος
sections.translation: the gods ’ abhorrence ; while lord Agamemnon married Clytemnestra , notorious in Hellas ; and we three daughters were born : Chrysothemis , Iphigenia , and myself , Electra ; also a son Orestes ; all from that one accursed mother ,

sections.label: Section 25
sections.original: ἣ πόσιν ἀπείρῳ περιβαλοῦσʼ ὑφάσματι
sections.translation: who slew her husband , after snaring him in an inextricable robe . Her reason a maiden ’ s lips may not declare , and so I leave it unclear for the world to guess at . What need for me to charge Phoebus with wrong-doing ? Though he persuaded Orestes

sections.label: Section 30
sections.original: κτεῖναι , πρὸς οὐχ ἅπαντας εὔκλειαν φέρον .
sections.translation: to slay his own mother , a deed that few approved . Still it was his obedience to the god that made him kill her ; I had a share in the murder , in so far as a woman could , and Pylades , who helped us to bring it about .

sections.label: Section 34
sections.original: ἐντεῦθεν ἀγρίᾳ συντακεὶς νόσῳ νοσεῖ
sections.translation: After this my poor Orestes , wasting away in a cruel disease ,

sections.label: Section 35
sections.original: τλήμων Ὀρέστης ὅδε πεσὼν ἐν δεμνίοις
sections.translation: lies fallen on his couch , and it is his mother ’ s blood that drives him round and round in frenzied fits ; I am ashamed to name the goddesses , whose terrors are chasing him — the Eumenides . It is now the sixth day

sections.label: Section 40
sections.original: θανοῦσα μήτηρ πυρὶ καθήγνισται δέμας ,
sections.translation: since the body of his murdered mother was committed to the cleansing fire ; since then no food has gone down his throat , nor has he washed his skin ; but wrapped in his cloak he weeps in his lucid moments , whenever the fever leaves him ;

sections.label: Section 45
sections.original: πηδᾷ δρομαῖος , πῶλος ὣς ὑπὸ ζυγοῦ .
sections.translation: at other times he bounds headlong from his couch , as a colt when it is loosed from the yoke . This city of Argos has decreed that no man give us shelter in home or hearth , or speak to matricides like us ; and this is the fateful day on which the Argives will take a vote ,

sections.label: Section 50
sections.original: εἰ χρὴ θανεῖν νὼ λευσίμῳ πετρώματι .
sections.translation: whether we are both to die by stoning . or to whet the steel and plunge it in our necks . There is , it is true , one hope of escape from death : Menelaus has landed from Troy ; his fleet now crowds the haven of Nauplia where he has come to anchor on the shore , returned at last from Troy

sections.label: Section 55
sections.original: ἀκταῖσιν ὁρμεῖ , δαρὸν ἐκ Τροίας χρόνον
sections.translation: after ceaseless wanderings ; but Helen , that so-called lady of sorrows , he has sent on to our palace , waiting for the night , lest any of those parents whose sons died at Troy might see her if she went by day , and set to stoning her .

sections.label: Section 60
sections.original: προύπεμψεν ἐς δῶμʼ ἡμέτερον · ἔστιν δʼ ἔσω
sections.translation: Within she sits , weeping for her sister and the calamities of her family , and yet she has still some solace in her woe ; for Hermione , the child she left at home when she sailed for Troy , the maid whom Menelaus brought from Sparta

sections.label: Section 65
sections.original: Μενέλαος ἀγαγὼν Ἑρμιόνην Σπάρτης ἄπο ,
sections.translation: and entrusted to my mother ’ s keeping , is still a cause of joy to her and a reason to forget her sorrows .

sections.label: Section 67
sections.original: βλέπω δὲ πᾶσαν εἰς ὁδόν , πότʼ ὄψομαι
sections.translation: I am watching each approach , until I see Menelaus arriving ; for unless we find some safety from him , we have only a feeble anchor to ride on otherwise .

sections.label: Section 70
sections.original: σωθῶμεν . ἄπορον χρῆμα δυστυχῶν δόμος .
sections.translation: A helpless thing , an unlucky house !

sections.label: Section 71
sections.original: ὦ παῖ Κλυταιμήστρας τε καὶ Ἀγαμέμνονος ,
sections.translation: Daughter of Clytemnestra and Agamemnon , unhappy Electra , a maiden for so long , how is it with you and your brother , this ill-starred Orestes who slew his mother ?

sections.label: Section 75
sections.original: προσφθέγμασιν γὰρ οὐ μιαίνομαι σέθεν ,
sections.translation: For referring the sin as I do to Phoebus , I incur no pollution by addressing you ; and yet I am truly sorry for the death of my sister Clytemnestra , whom I never saw after I was driven by heaven-sent frenzy to sail as I did to Ilium ;

sections.label: Section 80
sections.original: οὐκ εἶδον , ἀπολειφθεῖσα δʼ αἰάζω τύχας .
sections.translation: but now that I am parted from her , I bewail our misfortunes .

sections.label: Section 81
sections.original: Ἑλένη , τί σοι λέγοιμʼ ἂν ἅ γε παροῦσʼ ὁρᾷς ;
sections.translation: Helen , why should I speak of that which your own eyes can see ? Agamemnon ’ s house in misfortune ?

sections.label: Section 83
sections.original: ἐγὼ μὲν ἄυπνος πάρεδρος ἀθλίῳ νεκρῷ
sections.translation: Beside his wretched corpse I sit , sleepless — for corpse he is , so faint his breath —

sections.label: Section 85
sections.original: θάσσω · τὰ τούτου δʼ οὐκ ὀνειδίζω κακά .
sections.translation: not that I reproach him with his sufferings ; but you are highly blessed and your husband too . you have come upon us in the hour of adversity

sections.label: Section 88
sections.original: πόσον χρόνον δʼ ἐν δεμνίοις πέπτωχʼ ὅδε ;
sections.translation: How long has he lain in this way on the couch ?

sections.label: Section 89
sections.original: ἐξ οὗπερ αἷμα γενέθλιον κατήνυσεν .
sections.translation: Ever since he spilt his mother ’ s blood .

sections.label: Section 90
sections.original: ὢ μέλεος · ἡ τεκοῦσά θʼ , ὡς διώλετο .
sections.translation: Unhappy wretch ! unhappy mother ! what a death she died .

sections.label: Section 91
sections.original: οὕτως ἔχει τάδʼ , ὥστʼ ἀπείρηκεν κακοῖς .
sections.translation: Unhappy enough to succumb to his misery .

sections.label: Section 92
sections.original: πρὸς θεῶν , πίθοιʼ ἂν δῆτά μοί τι , παρθένε ;
sections.translation: By the gods , would you hear me a moment , maiden ?

sections.label: Section 93
sections.original: ὡς ἄσχολός γε συγγόνου προσεδρίᾳ .
sections.translation: Yes , with such leisure as this watching over a brother leaves .

sections.label: Section 94
sections.original: βούλῃ τάφον μοι πρὸς κασιγνήτης μολεῖν ;
sections.translation: Will you go for me to my sister ’ s tomb ?

sections.label: Section 95
sections.original: μητρὸς κελεύεις τῆς ἐμῆς ; τίνος χάριν ;
sections.translation: Would you have me seek my mother ’ s tomb ? Why ?

sections.label: Section 96
sections.original: κόμης ἀπαρχὰς καὶ χοὰς φέρουσʼ ἐμάς .
sections.translation: To carry an offering of hair and a libation from me .

sections.label: Section 97
sections.original: σοὶ δʼ οὐχὶ θεμιτὸν πρὸς φίλων στείχειν τάφον ;
sections.translation: Isn ’ t it right for you to go to the tomb of one you love ?

sections.label: Section 98
sections.original: δεῖξαι γὰρ Ἀργείοισι σῶμʼ αἰσχύνομαι .
sections.translation: No , for I am ashamed to show myself in Argos .

sections.label: Section 99
sections.original: ὀψέ γε φρονεῖς εὖ , τότε λιποῦσʼ αἰσχρῶς δόμους .
sections.translation: A late repentance surely for one who left her home so shamefully then .

sections.label: Section 100
sections.original: ὀρθῶς ἔλεξας , οὐ φίλως δʼ ἐμοὶ λέγεις .
sections.translation: You have told the truth , but your telling is not kind to me .

sections.label: Section 101
sections.original: αἰδὼς δὲ δὴ τίς σʼ ἐς Μυκηναίους ἔχει ;
sections.translation: What is this supposed shame before the eyes of Mycenae that possesses you ?

sections.label: Section 102
sections.original: δέδοικα πατέρας τῶν ὑπʼ Ἰλίῳ νεκρῶν .
sections.translation: I am afraid of the fathers of those who lie dead at Ilium .

sections.label: Section 103
sections.original: δεινὸν γάρ · Ἄργει τʼ ἀναβοᾷ διὰ στόμα .
sections.translation: Good cause for fear ; your name is on every tongue in Argos .

sections.label: Section 104
sections.original: σύ νυν χάριν μοι τὸν φόβον λύσασα δός .
sections.translation: Then free me of my fear and grant me this favor .

sections.label: Section 105
sections.original: οὐκ ἂν δυναίμην μητρὸς ἐσβλέψαι τάφον .
sections.translation: I could not bear to look upon my mother ’ s grave .

sections.label: Section 106
sections.original: αἰσχρόν γε μέντοι προσπόλους φέρειν τάδε .
sections.translation: And yet it would be shame indeed for servants to bear these offerings .

sections.label: Section 107
sections.original: τί δʼ οὐχὶ θυγατρὸς Ἑρμιόνης πέμπεις δέμας ;
sections.translation: Then why not send your daughter Hermione ?

sections.label: Section 108
sections.original: ἐς ὄχλον ἕρπειν παρθένοισιν οὐ καλόν .
sections.translation: It is not good for maidens to go into a crowd .

sections.label: Section 109
sections.original: καὶ μὴν τίνοι γʼ ἂν τῇ τεθνηκυίᾳ τροφάς .
sections.translation: And yet she would be repaying her dead foster-mother ’ s care .

sections.label: Section 110
sections.original: ὀρθῶς ἔλεξας , πείθομαί τέ σοι , κόρη .
sections.translation: You have told the truth and have convinced me , maiden . Yes , I will send my daughter for you are right . Calling . Hermione , my child , come out , before the palace . Take these libations and these tresses of mine in your hands , and go pour round Clytemnestra ’ s tomb

sections.label: Section 115
sections.original: μελίκρατʼ ἄφες γάλακτος οἰνωπόν τʼ ἄχνην ,
sections.translation: a mingled cup of honey , milk , and frothing wine ; then stand upon the heaped-up grave , and say this : Helen , your sister , sends you these libations as her gift , fearing herself to approach your tomb from terror of the Argive mob and bid her harbor kindly thoughts

sections.label: Section 120
sections.original: ἐμοί τε καὶ σοὶ καὶ πόσει γνώμην ἔχειν
sections.translation: towards me and you and my husband ; towards these two wretched sufferers , too , whom the gods have destroyed . And promise that I will pay in full whatever funeral gifts are due from me to a sister . Now go , my child , and hurry ;

sections.label: Section 125
sections.original: δοῦσʼ ὡς τάχιστα τῆς πάλιν μέμνησʼ ὁδοῦ .
sections.translation: and soon as you have made the libations at the tomb , think of your return . Exit Helen .

sections.label: Section 126
sections.original: ὦ φύσις , ἐν ἀνθρώποισιν ὡς μέγʼ εἶ κακόν ,
sections.translation: O human nature , how great an evil you are in men ! and what salvation , too , to those who have a goodly heritage there .

sections.label: Section 128
sections.original: εἴδετε παρʼ ἄκρας ὡς ἀπέθρισεν τρίχας ,
sections.translation: Did you see how she cut off her hair only at the ends , to preserve its beauty ? She is the same woman as of old .

sections.label: Section 130
sections.original: θεοί σε μισήσειαν , ὥς μʼ ἀπώλεσας
sections.translation: May the gods hate you ! for you have proved the ruin of me and my brother and all Hellas .

sections.label: Section 132
sections.original: αἵδʼ αὖ πάρεισι τοῖς ἐμοῖς θρηνήμασι
sections.translation: Alas ! here are my friends once more , coming to unite their plaintive dirge with mine ; they will soon put an end to my brother ’ s peaceful sleep , and cause my tears to flow

sections.label: Section 135
sections.original: δακρύοις , ἀδελφὸν ὅταν ὁρῶ μεμηνότα .
sections.translation: when I see him in frenzy .

sections.label: Section 136
sections.original: ὦ φίλταται γυναῖκες , ἡσύχῳ ποδὶ
sections.translation: Dearest friends , step softly ; not a sound ; not a whisper ! For though this kindness of yours is well-meant , rouse him and I shall rue it .

sections.label: Section 1.140
sections.original: σῖγα σῖγα , λεπτὸν ἴχνος ἀρβύλης
sections.translation: Hush , hush ! let your footsteps fall lightly ! not a sound !

sections.label: Section 1.142
sections.original: ἀποπρὸ βᾶτʼ ἐκεῖσʼ , ἀποπρό μοι κοίτας .
sections.translation: Go further from his couch , further , I beseech you !

sections.label: Section 1.143
sections.original: ἰδού , πείθομαι .
sections.translation: There , I obey .

sections.label: Section 1.145
sections.original: ἆ ἆ σύριγγος ὅπως πνοὰ
sections.translation: Ah , ah ! Speak like the breath of a slender reed-pipe , my dear , I pray .
